checks.scenario: You run `pip install anthropic` on your laptop to start a new AI project. Three weeks later, a teammate clones the repo and runs the same command. They get a different version of the SDK because a new release shipped overnight.
checks.question: Which uv workflow prevents this class of environment drift?
options: A. Pin the version manually in requirements.txt with `anthropic==0.40.0`
B. Use `uv add anthropic` which generates uv.lock, then commit uv.lock to the repo
C. Use `uv sync --frozen` which prevents any package installation
D. Store .venv/ in the repository so teammates use your exact binaries
checks.explanation: `uv add anthropic` updates pyproject.toml and regenerates uv.lock with every transitive dependency pinned exactly. When your teammate runs `uv sync`, they get bit-for-bit the same packages. requirements.txt pins only the direct dep, not transitive ones. --frozen prevents installs rather than enabling reproducibility. Committing .venv/ is bad practice and platform-specific.

checks.scenario: You have three AI projects on your laptop: project-a (Python 3.11, anthropic 0.38), project-b (Python 3.12, anthropic 0.42), and project-c (Python 3.11, anthropic 0.40). You switch between them throughout the day.
checks.question: What is the correct way to run each project's scripts with uv?
options: A. Activate the venv for each project with `source .venv/bin/activate` before running scripts
B. Use `uv run python script.py` from inside each project directory
C. Set PYTHONPATH to each project's .venv/lib directory before running
D. Install all three anthropic versions into a shared venv with `--target`
checks.explanation: `uv run` automatically detects the nearest pyproject.toml and uv.lock, selects the correct Python version, and injects the right venv -- all without any manual activation. You just `cd` to the project and run. Activating venvs manually works but breaks silently if you forget to switch; PYTHONPATH manipulation is fragile; shared venvs cannot hold multiple versions of the same package.

checks.scenario: You are setting up CI/CD for your AI project on GitHub Actions. Your pipeline needs to install dependencies and run tests. The pipeline should fail if anyone committed changes to pyproject.toml without updating uv.lock.
checks.question: Which uv command should your CI pipeline use to install dependencies?
options: A. `uv add -r requirements.txt`
B. `uv sync`
C. `uv sync --frozen`
D. `uv install --all`
checks.explanation: `uv sync --frozen` installs exactly what is in uv.lock and errors if pyproject.toml and uv.lock are out of sync. This catches the case where a developer ran `uv add` but forgot to commit the updated lockfile. Plain `uv sync` would silently update the lockfile. There is no `uv add -r` or `uv install --all` command.

checks.scenario: A new engineer joins your team and needs to run the project. They clone the repo and see `pyproject.toml`, `uv.lock`, and `.venv/` listed (the previous engineer accidentally committed .venv/). They ask you: which files should actually be committed and which should not?
checks.question: What is the correct answer?
options: A. Commit pyproject.toml and .venv/; uv.lock is auto-generated and should be in .gitignore
B. Commit pyproject.toml and uv.lock; .venv/ should be in .gitignore
C. Commit all three: pyproject.toml, uv.lock, and .venv/ for maximum reproducibility
D. Commit only uv.lock; pyproject.toml is auto-generated from it
checks.explanation: pyproject.toml is the human-edited dependency manifest. uv.lock is the machine-generated exact pin file -- it must be committed so teammates get reproducible installs. .venv/ contains platform-specific compiled binaries that are not portable across OS/architecture; it belongs in .gitignore. Anyone cloning the repo runs `uv sync` to recreate .venv/ locally.

checks.scenario: You need to run a one-off script with ruff (a Python linter) to check your code, but you do not want to add ruff as a project dependency since it is only needed for CI.
checks.question: What is the idiomatic uv command for this?
options: A. `pip install ruff && ruff check .`
B. `uv add --dev ruff && uv run ruff check .`
C. `uvx ruff check .`
D. `uv tool install ruff && ruff check .`
checks.explanation: `uvx ruff check .` (shorthand for `uv tool run ruff check .`) runs ruff in an isolated temporary environment without adding it to pyproject.toml. It is the equivalent of `npx` for Node. `uv add --dev ruff` would add it to the project's dev dependencies, which is appropriate for team-wide linting but heavier than a one-off run. `uv tool install` makes it available globally, which is also valid but more permanent than needed here.

checks.scenario: Your project requires Python 3.11 specifically, but `uv run python --version` shows Python 3.12.3. The `.python-version` file contains `3.11`. You did not install Python 3.11 on your machine.
checks.question: What happens when you run `uv run python main.py`?
options: A. uv uses the system Python 3.12 and ignores .python-version
B. uv raises an error and exits with a message about the version mismatch
C. uv automatically downloads and installs Python 3.11, then runs the script with it
D. uv creates a new virtual environment with Python 3.12 and warns about the mismatch
checks.explanation: uv manages Python versions itself. When .python-version specifies a version not currently installed, uv fetches and installs it automatically before running the script. This is one of the key advantages over the old pyenv + pip stack: you do not need to separately install Python versions or run `pyenv install 3.11`.

checks.scenario: You are debugging a production AI issue and need to quickly test a hypothesis in a throwaway script. You want to use httpx for the HTTP call but do not want to add it permanently to your production project.
checks.question: What is the cleanest way to run a one-off script with httpx using uv?
options: A. `uv add httpx`, run the script, then `uv remove httpx`
B. `uv run --with httpx python debug_script.py`
C. Create a new virtual environment, install httpx, run the script, delete the environment
D. `pip install httpx` into the system Python and run the script
checks.explanation: `uv run --with httpx python debug_script.py` injects httpx into the run environment without modifying pyproject.toml or uv.lock. The project itself is unaffected. This is the correct pattern for debugging scripts and one-off tools. The add/remove approach works but is noisy (it updates version control files). Creating a new venv manually defeats the purpose of uv.
